attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for routine follow-up of his bronchiectasis. He was diagnosed four years ago following recurrent chest infections. Despite extensive investigation including immunoglobulin levels, sweat chloride testing, alpha-1 antitrypsin levels, and screening for primary ciliary dyskinesia, no underlying cause was identified. Over the past year, he has had one exacerbation requiring oral antibiotics. He experiences daily productive cough with yellow-green sputum that he finds difficult to clear. His medical history includes idiopathic bronchiectasis. He does not take any regular medications and has completed all recommended vaccinations including annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccines. He attends regular chest physiotherapy sessions.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Chest examination reveals scattered coarse crackles bilaterally. His most recent spirometry shows FEV1 78% predicted with minimal reversibility.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following should be tried next?
attributes.explanation:
This young adult with idiopathic bronchiectasis has persistent symptoms including difficulty clearing sputum and one exacerbation in the past year despite appropriate baseline management including vaccinations and physiotherapy. The next step should focus on improving mucociliary clearance and reducing exacerbation frequency.

Hypertonic saline is the most appropriate consideration. Hypertonic saline (typically 3-7%) nebulisation has been shown to improve mucociliary clearance, reduce sputum viscosity, and decrease exacerbation rates in patients with bronchiectasis. This would directly address his difficulty clearing sputum. However, before commencing regular therapy, a supervised hypertonic saline challenge must be performed to assess tolerability and risk of bronchospasm.

Inhaled budesonide/formoterol would be inappropriate. Long-acting beta-agonists (LABAs) have limited role in bronchiectasis management unless there is evidence of significant reversible airway obstruction. This patient's spirometry shows minimal reversibility, indicating that bronchodilator therapy would likely provide little benefit. Inhaled corticosteroids are only indicated in bronchiectasis if there is another specific indication such as coexistent asthma or allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis. This patient has no evidence of asthma or other conditions that would warrant inhaled corticosteroid therapy. Although budesonide/ formoterol is an effective therapy in asthma, it's use in bronchiectasis is overall nor recommended.

Regular azithromycin could be considered for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, but current guidelines recommend macrolide therapy only for patients with three or more exacerbations per year despite optimal management. This patient has had one exacerbation in the past year, not meeting this threshold.

Inhaled N-acetylcysteine has limited evidence in non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis. While mucolytics like dornase alfa show benefit in cystic fibrosis, their efficacy in non-CF bronchiectasis is not established, and N-acetylcysteine specifically has not shown consistent benefit.

Regular ciprofloxacin is not recommended as prophylactic therapy due to the risk of developing antimicrobial resistance. Fluoroquinolones should be reserved for treating acute exacerbations caused by susceptible organisms.
learningPoints: Hypertonic saline nebulisation improves mucociliary clearance and reduces exacerbations in patients with bronchiectasis.
Long-term macrolide therapy is reserved for patients with three or more exacerbations per year despite optimal management.
Inhaled corticosteroids are only indicated in bronchiectasis if there is another specific indication such as coexistent asthma or allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis.
references: Chang AB, Bell SC, Byrnes CA, Dawkins P, Holland AE, Kennedy E, et al. Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) position statement on chronic suppurative lung disease and bronchiectasis in children, adolescents and adults in Australia and New Zealand. Respirol Carlton Vic. 2023 Apr;28(4):339–49.
Hill A.T., Sullivan A.L., Chalmers J.D., De Soyza A., Stuart Elborn J., Andres Floto R., et al. British Thoracic Society guideline for bronchiectasis in adults. Thorax. 2019;74.
Polverino E, Goeminne PC, McDonnell MJ, Aliberti S, Marshall SE, Loebinger MR, et al. European Respiratory Society guidelines for the management of adult bronchiectasis. Eur Respir J. 2017;50(3).
